claim: A μFTIR study with a 3 μm method limit reported 39 microplastic particles in 11 of 13 human lung-tissue samples; blank-adjusted results were also reported.
locators: Abstract; n=13, 3 μm size limit, results and blank adjustments
caveat: Small selected series. Detection supports an inhalation route; it does not establish disease, population prevalence or a causal effect.

claim: In a prospective observational study, polyethylene was detected in 150 of 257 excised carotid plaques and PVC in 31; the population had undergone carotid endarterectomy.
caveat: This selected surgical cohort is not a prevalence sample. Detection is not proof that the material caused disease.

claim: Within that carotid-surgery cohort, plaque detection was associated with a higher composite rate of myocardial infarction, stroke or all-cause death over a mean 33.7 months (adjusted hazard ratio 4.53; 95% CI 2.00–10.27).

scope.outcome: composite cardiovascular event or all-cause death

caveat: Association is not causality. Residual confounding, selected population and analytical uncertainty remain material.

claim: A postmortem study reported micro- and nanoplastic signals in decedent kidney, liver and brain using complementary analytical methods.

scope.polymer: primarily PE in the reported analyses

caveat: Postmortem detection does not establish exposure history, living burden, disease causation or clinical actionability. Quantitative cross-tissue and dementia interpretations remain method-sensitive.

claim: A single category-level conclusion that microplastics cause human disease remains contested: reviews differ in how they integrate limited human observations with animal evidence.
locators: Chartres et al. Abstract > included-study counts and conclusions
caveat: One review reaches suspected-harm conclusions for selected outcomes using human and animal evidence; another emphasizes that small human samples and inadequate exposure assessment preclude adequate risk assessment. The different frameworks are not averaged into a certainty score.

claim: Microplastic results depend on sampling, preparation, contamination control, size threshold, polymer identification and whether a method reports particle count, morphology or polymer mass.
caveat: Method characteristics demonstrated in water or reference materials cannot be assumed to transfer unchanged to blood or tissue.

claim: In a 22-laboratory simulated clean-water study, FTIR and Raman identified polymer type correctly for 95% and 91% of analyzed particles, while blank contamination averaged 91 particles with a standard deviation of 141.

scope.particle_range: 1 μm to >500 μm; strongest recovery >50 μm

caveat: The strong identification result coexists with substantial blank contamination; this clean-water result is not a human-biospecimen validation.

claim: A 2025 VAMAS comparison across 84 laboratories found wide reproducibility ranges for thermo-analytical and spectroscopic measurements even in a simplified matrix with 10–200 μm PE and PET reference materials.
locators: Abstract > materials, methods and reproducibility ranges
caveat: The metric and matrix are specific to this study. Do not translate the percentages into a universal laboratory error rate.

claim: NIST identifies the lack of well-characterized reference materials as a barrier to repeatable and reproducible micro- and nanoplastic measurements.
locators: Reference Materials and opening problem statement
caveat: NIST research activity is not evidence that universal certified materials or human assays are already available.

claim: ISO 24187:2023 defines principles and minimum requirements for environmental microplastics analysis; it does not impose monitoring requirements or validate human-biospecimen testing.
locators: ISO catalog Abstract and General Information
caveat: Environmental standardization should not be described as clinical assay validation.

claim: California adopted a drinking-water testing and reporting method framework in 2022, creating a matrix-specific laboratory and monitoring demand signal without establishing a health-based limit.
caveat: This is a California water-policy framework, not a federal mandate, clinical standard or health threshold.

claim: EPA's April 2026 draft CCL 6 includes microplastics as a drinking-water research priority and names four gaps: health-based definition, validated analytical method, mixture interpretation and source attribution.
caveat: Draft candidate-list status is not a monitoring mandate, national drinking-water limit or finding of population-level harm.

claim: Working inference v0.1: draft CCL 6 made near-term U.S. national drinking-water monitoring for microplastics likely.
locators: 91 FR 17186 > CCL process and Microplastics section
caveat: The underlying notice explicitly imposed no requirement. This inference was not a regulatory fact and is preserved only to show why the later UCMR 6 proposal changed the thesis.

claim: EPA's July 2026 proposed UCMR 6 omits microplastics because no validated EPA or consensus drinking-water method could meet required quality-control, accuracy, precision and statutory timing needs; EPA points to a future cycle after methods and laboratory capacity mature.
caveat: UCMR 6 is proposed, not final. Omission does not mean microplastics are safe or irrelevant; it identifies measurement and laboratory capacity as the current gate.

claim: EU Regulation 2023/2055 restricts intentionally added synthetic polymer microparticles within defined scope, exclusions and sector-specific transition periods.
caveat: It is not a blanket ban on all plastics, secondary microplastics, tire wear or textile fragmentation.

claim: ARPA-H's STOMP roadmap stages measurement and biological understanding before a later removal technical area; the initial solicitation did not solicit removal TA3.
locators: STOMP > The Solution and Technical Areas
caveat: A program objective is not achieved performance. Public materials differ on exact phase timing, so no precise duration is asserted.

claim: ARPA-H committed a $144 million program envelope to STOMP and anticipated multiple awards; this is not evidence that $144 million has already been awarded.
locators: ARPA-H launch > opening and program commitment
caveat: No predetermined per-award budget is stated. Commitment, solicitation and award are distinct states.

claim: Working inference v0.1: no published human procedure had shown any measurable reduction in circulating microplastic signal.
locators: STOMP > The Problem; no known validated removal
caveat: The source addressed validated removal, not an exhaustive literature absence. A May 2026 uncontrolled study superseded this narrower working inference.

claim: An uncontrolled 2026 plasma-exchange study reported lower post-procedure circulating particle counts in higher-baseline groups, while the largest low-baseline group increased; the result is a developing assay signal, not validated body-burden removal.

scope.polymer: not orthogonally resolved in the assay

locators: Methods; Results Table 2; Discussion; Limitations; conflicts of interest
caveat: No randomized control, independent public assay validation, tissue or total-body measurement, durability or health outcome. Procedure materials may add particles, and repeated procedures are not independent participants.

claim: No cited study establishes safe, durable reduction of total human tissue/body burden with improved health outcomes; validated biomedical removal remains an unfilled evidence rung.
locators: STOMP > The Problem and program sequence
Weinstein et al. Discussion and Limitations
caveat: This boundary is dated to the reviewed source set and should be re-reviewed when independent controlled results or STOMP TA3 evidence appears.

claim: NCT07658443 is an estimated 20-person, prospective observational paired-biomarker study of clinically prescribed DFPP; no results were posted at verification.

scope.particle_range: primary assay claims down to approximately 50 nm

locators: Study Overview; Design; Outcome Measures; Enrollment; Record Dates
caveat: No results are posted; treatment is not assigned by the study, and the endpoint is a biomarker rather than durable burden or health benefit.

claim: The exposome is broader than microplastics and spans environmental influences across the life course; NIEHS names measurement, harmonization, rigor and atlas feasibility as research priorities.
caveat: A strategic priority is not proof of a unified market, clinical actionability or a dedicated microplastics budget.

claim: Current evidence supports human detection and selected associations, but not a general claim that microplastics as one category cause a named human disease.
caveat: Lack of general causal proof is not proof of safety. Particle-, route-, dose- and outcome-specific relationships may differ.

claim: Delphi inference: measurement, reference materials and reproducibility infrastructure should lead current sourcing because verified public programs and standards expose unmet comparability gates.
evidence_type: investment inference from public demand and technical gaps
caveat: Public need and funding signals do not prove venture-scale margins, willingness to pay for a particular product or a defensible moat.

claim: Delphi inference: prevention and safer-material substitution can build conviction where a defined regulation, specification or operating cost creates a buyer before broad human causal proof.
evidence_type: investment inference from scoped compliance signals
caveat: Compliance demand can become commodity service revenue; technical capture, lifecycle proof, maintenance burden and customer economics remain company-specific diligence gates.

claim: Delphi inference: clinical interpretation should remain selective until an assay is analytically valid, linked to reference ranges and shown to change a useful decision or outcome.
evidence_type: investment gate derived from human evidence limits
caveat: This is a diligence posture, not a claim that a particular clinical product is safe, effective or approved.

claim: Delphi inference: biomedical removal remains Watch until independent evidence connects a relevant burden to safe, durable clearance and patient benefit.
evidence_type: investment gate derived from intervention limits

scope.matrix_or_tissue: blood, tissue and total body as relevant

caveat: A new controlled, independently measured result could re-rate this posture; current self-pay activity is not payer adoption or validation.

claim: Counter-thesis: the umbrella category may fragment by particle, route, matrix and buyer; regulation can create compliance work without creating one scalable venture market.
locators: Abstract > 22-laboratory design, clean-water matrix and variability
Abstract > 84-laboratory VAMAS design and reproducibility ranges
ISO catalog Abstract > environmental analytical-principles scope
Actions; Testing and Reporting > California drinking-water scope
Scope; Exclusions; Transition periods > intentionally added particles
Exposomics definition and research priorities > measurement and harmonization
caveat: This is a falsifiable failure scenario, not a forecast. Delphi should demand a defined user, decision, budget and repeatable economics for each layer.

claim: Microplastics have been detected across the global environment, from Antarctic and Arctic regions to the peak of Mount Everest and the depths of the Mariana Trench, and human exposure occurs mainly through ingestion and inhalation.
evidence_type: review synthesis of environmental distribution and human exposure

scope.matrix_or_tissue: environmental media and human exposure routes

locators: Li et al. Introduction; environmental distribution
WHO Overview; dietary and inhalation exposure
caveat: Widespread environmental presence and human exposure do not by themselves establish population-level disease or a causal effect.
